key_insight: A microscopic constitutive model couples nonaffine elasticity with irreversible relaxation to explain stress overshoot, yielding, steady flow, and polymer strain hardening in amorphous solids.
engineering_relevance: It offers a unified, physically grounded way to predict nonlinear stress--strain response of metallic and polymer glasses across wide strain rates with few parameters.
method: Model nonaffine elastic response with strain-induced loss of mechanical connectivity.
Couple elasticity to stretched-exponential many-body relaxation to capture viscous dissipation and yielding.
Include finite chain extensibility to reproduce polymer strain hardening at large deformation.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports quantitative agreement with experimental stress--strain curves for metallic glasses, polycarbonate, PMMA, and epoxy resins over a broad range of strain rates.
watch_item: Parameter identification depends on independently measured relaxation exponents and the model may be sensitive to material-specific calibration outside the tested glass classes.
paper_title: Microscopic constitutive theory of stress overshoot, yielding, and strain hardening in amorphous materials
